title: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease
authors: F. Richard Bringhurst, Henry M. Kronenberg, Eva S. Liu, Marc N. Wein

items.subtopic: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Bone Remodeling: The Roles of Osteoblasts, Osteoclasts, and Osteocytes
items.content: Bone remodeling is a dynamic process involving osteoblasts that produce bone matrix, osteoclasts that resorb the matrix, and osteocytes that coordinate these activities. This coordination ensures the maintenance of bone strength and mineral homeostasis.
keyPoints: Osteoblasts synthesize and secrete organic matrix; they regulate its mineralization.
Osteoclasts are responsible for resorbing the bone matrix.
Osteocytes, derived from osteoblasts, act as mechanosensors and master regulators of bone formation and resorption.
The skeleton is highly vascular, receiving ~10% of cardiac output.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)

items.subtopic: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)
items.title: Significance of Type I Collagen and Genetic Factors in Bone Structure
items.content: Type I collagen, comprising 90-95% of the organic matrix, is crucial for bone structure. Mutations in COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes can lead to osteogenesis imperfecta, highlighting the importance of collagen's structural integrity.
keyPoints: Type I collagen forms ~90–95% of the organic matrix.
Mutations in COL1A1 or COL1A2 disrupt bone organization leading to osteogenesis imperfecta.
Runx2 is a key transcription factor regulating osteoblast development and expression of critical proteins.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)

items.subtopic: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)
items.title: The Role of Genetic Regulation in Osteoblast Development
items.content: Runx2 is essential for osteoblast development, influencing the expression of several proteins necessary for bone formation. Its regulation by BMPs and its impact on conditions like dedocranial dysplasia underscore its importance.
keyPoints: Runx2 regulates osteoblast protein expression including osterix, osteopontin, and type I collagen.
BMPs partially regulate Runx2 expression.
Runx2-deficient mice lack osteoblasts; heterozygous mutations cause dedocranial dysplasia.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)

items.title: Role of Indian Hedgehog in Osteoblast Development
items.content: Indian hedgehog (Ihh) is crucial for osteoblast development, particularly in endochondral ossification. Ihh-deficient mice lack osteoblasts in this type of bone formation.
keyPoints: Ihh is essential for osteoblast development in endochondral ossification.
Ihh-deficient mice exhibit a lack of osteoblasts in bones formed on cartilage molds.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 2)

items.title: Regulation of Osteoclast Activity
items.content: Osteoclast activity is regulated by RANK ligand and osteoprotegerin. RANK ligand promotes osteoclast differentiation, while osteoprotegerin inhibits it.
keyPoints: RANK ligand binds to the RANK receptor on osteoclast progenitors, stimulating their differentiation and activation.
Osteoprotegerin acts as a decoy receptor for RANK ligand, inhibiting osteoclast differentiation.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 2)

items.title: Mechanism of Osteoclast-Mediated Bone Resorption
items.content: Osteoclasts resorb bone in Howship's lacunae, forming a tight seal and secreting protons and proteinases to dissolve the mineral phase.
keyPoints: Osteoclasts attach to bone matrix via $\alpha\nu\beta3$ integrin.
Proton pump ATPase at the ruffled border secretes acid to solubilize the mineral phase.
Carbonic anhydrase type II generates protons for resorption.
Cathepsin K acts as a protease in the acidic environment.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 2)

items.title: Bone Remodeling Dynamics
items.content: Bone remodeling is a continuous process involving resorption by osteoclasts and formation by osteoblasts. In adults, approximately 4% of trabecular bone surface is actively resorbed, while 10-15% is covered with osteoid, the unmineralized new bone formed by osteoblasts.
keyPoints: Adults have ~4% active resorption on trabecular bone surfaces.
10–15% of trabecular surfaces are covered with osteoid.
18% of total skeletal calcium is deposited and removed annually.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 3)

items.title: Mechanisms of Bone Response to Mechanical Stress
items.content: Bone remodeling occurs along lines of mechanical stress. Osteocytes sense these stresses and regulate osteoclasts and osteoblast activity, with signals such as sclerostin modulating bone formation.
keyPoints: Osteocytes transmit mechanical stress signals to osteoclasts and osteoblasts.
Sclerostin inhibits wnt signaling, reducing bone formation.
Mechanical forces suppress sclerostin production, enhancing bone formation.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 3)

items.title: Markers of Bone Metabolic Activity
items.content: Assessment of osteoblast and osteoclast activity can aid in diagnosing bone diseases. Serum markers include bone-specific alkaline phosphatase, osteocalcin, and aminoterminal procollagen I fragments for bone formation; collagen degradation products are used to assess osteoclast activity.
keyPoints: Serum bone-specific alkaline phosphatase indicates osteoblast activity.
Osteocalcin is secreted by osteoblasts, marking their activity.
Aminoterminal procollagen I fragments index bone formation.
Collagen degradation products measure osteoclast activity.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 3)

items.title: Importance of Ionized Calcium Concentration
items.content: Ionized calcium concentration in the extracellular fluid is critical for cellular functions, particularly neuromuscular activity and signal transduction. It must be maintained within a narrow range due to its significant role.
keyPoints: Ionized calcium concentration in ECF: 1.1–1.3 mmol/L
Cytosolic free calcium levels: ~100 nmol/L, 10,000-fold lower than ionized calcium in blood
Total blood calcium concentration: 2.2–2.6 mM (8.5–10.5 mg/dL), with ~50% ionized
Adjustments for protein changes in total serum calcium: +0.8 times deficit in albumin, +0.5 times deficit in immunoglobulin
Best practice: Measure blood ionized calcium directly using calcium-selective electrodes during acute illness
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 1)

items.title: Regulation of Ionized Calcium by Hormones
items.content: The regulation of ionized calcium concentration in the extracellular fluid is primarily mediated through hormonal adjustments involving PTH and 1,25(OH)2D. These hormones influence calcium movement across intestinal and renal epithelia and bone.
keyPoints: PTH secretion suppressed by blood ionized calcium via CaSRs on parathyroid cells
Mechanisms: reduces PTH mRNA levels, promotes cleavage to inactive peptides, suppresses release of PTH-containing granules
1,25(OH)2D inhibits PTH production through negative feedback
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 1)

items.title: Optimal Calcium Absorption Mechanisms
items.content: Calcium absorption in the intestine involves both passive and active mechanisms. Passive absorption, which is nonsaturable, accounts for about 5% of dietary intake, while active absorption via TRPV channels can account for 20-70%, influenced by 1,25(OH)₂D levels.
keyPoints: Passive calcium absorption approximates 5% of daily intake and is nonsaturable.
Active absorption involves apical entry through TRPV5 (kidney) and TRPV6 (intestine), controlled by 1,25(OH)₂D.
Active transport accounts for 20-70% of dietary calcium absorption.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 2)

items.title: Regulation and Excretion of Calcium
items.content: The body maintains a relatively constant daily net calcium absorption (~5–10 mmol/d) despite dietary variations, with renal excretion tightly regulated by ionized blood calcium levels. The kidney reabsorbs most filtered calcium in the proximal tubules and cTAL via paracellular mechanisms.
keyPoints: Daily net calcium absorption is ~5–10 mmol/d (200–400 mg/d).
Approximately 8-10 g/d of calcium is filtered by glomeruli, with only 2-3% appearing in urine.
65% reabsorbed in proximal tubules via paracellular route; cTAL reabsorbs ~20%, regulated by CaSR.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 2)

items.title: Consequences of Extreme Calcium Intake
items.content: At low calcium intake (<5 mmol/d), the body compensates through increased PTH and 1,25(OH)₂D to enhance bone resorption. High intake (>100 mmol/d) can lead to hypercalciuria and nephrocalcinosis due to passive absorption overwhelming regulatory mechanisms.
keyPoints: Sustained intake <5 mmol/d (<200 mg/d) leads to increased PTH and 1,25(OH)₂D, causing bone resorption.
High calcium intake (>100 mmol/d) can cause hypercalciuria, nephrocalcinosis, and hypercalcemia (milk-alkali syndrome).
Regulatory mechanisms include hormonal adjustments by PTH and vitamin D.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Serum Phosphate Variability and Measurement Timing
items.content: Serum phosphate levels are subject to significant daily variation due to dietary intake and circadian rhythms, with a typical nadir occurring between 7 and 10 A.M. This variability necessitates that serum phosphate measurements be performed in the basal, fasting state for accuracy.
keyPoints: Normal range of serum phosphate: 0.75–1.45 mmol/L (2.5–4.5 mg/dL)
Serum phosphate can vary by as much as 50% on a normal day
Carbohydrate administration, especially IV dextrose, can decrease serum phosphate significantly (>0.7 mmol/L or 2 mg/dL)
Measurements should be performed in the basal, fasting state to account for variability
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · PHOSPHORUS METABOLISM

items.title: Regulation of Renal Phosphate Reabsorption
items.content: Renal phosphate reabsorption is primarily regulated by changes in the expression and activity of Na⁺/PO₄²⁻ co-transporters (NaPi-2a and NaPi-2c) in the proximal tubule, influenced by PTH and FGF23. PTH reduces transporter levels rapidly, while FGF23 inhibits phosphate reabsorption and decreases 1,25(OH)₂D synthesis.
keyPoints: Renal tubular reabsorption of filtered phosphate load: ~4–6 g/d
Fractional excretion of phosphate: 10–15%
PTH reduces apical expression of Na⁺/PO₄²⁻ co-transporters in the proximal tubule
FGF23 inhibits phosphate reabsorption and decreases synthesis of 1,25(OH)₂D
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · PHOSPHORUS METABOLISM

items.title: Factors Affecting Renal Phosphate Reabsorption
items.content: Renal phosphate reabsorption is influenced by various factors including hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia, severe hypophosphatemia, ECF volume status, and renal insufficiency. These conditions can impair or enhance phosphate clearance.
keyPoints: Hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia, and severe hypophosphatemia impair renal phosphate reabsorption
ECF volume expansion enhances phosphate clearance; dehydration impairs it
Phosphate retention is a key feature of renal insufficiency
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · PHOSPHORUS METABOLISM

items.title: Understanding PTH/PTHrP-dependent Renal Tubular Phosphate Wasting
items.content: Hypophosphatemia can result from excessive renal phosphate excretion due to activation of PTH/PTHrP receptors in the proximal tubule. This is commonly seen in conditions like primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism and malignancy-associated hypercalcemia.
keyPoints: Primary hyperparathyroidism, secondary hyperparathyroidism (due to vitamin D deficiency/resistance, calcium starvation/malabsorption, Bartter's syndrome, autosomal recessive renal hypercalciuria with hypomagnesemia), and PTHrP-dependent hypercalcemia of malignancy are common causes.
Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia is a rare genetic disorder causing similar effects.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 1)

items.title: Role of FGF23 in PTH/PTHrP-independent Phosphate Wasting
items.content: FGF23 is a key regulator of phosphate metabolism, decreasing renal phosphate reabsorption and suppressing 1α-hydroxylase for vitamin D synthesis. Disorders like X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets (XLH) involve elevated FGF23 due to genetic mutations.
keyPoints: FGF23 is synthesized by osteoblast lineage cells, primarily osteocytes.
High-phosphate diets increase FGF23 levels; low-phosphate diets decrease them.
X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets (XLH) results from activating mutations in the gene encoding FGF23.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 1)

items.title: Acquired and Genetic Disorders Leading to Tubular Phosphate Wasting
items.content: Several genetic disorders, such as X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets and autosomal recessive syndromes like those involving DMP1 and ENPP1 mutations, cause elevated FGF23 levels leading to hypophosphatemia.
keyPoints: Tumor-induced osteomalacia (TIO) is an acquired disorder where tumors secrete FGF23, causing hypophosphatemia that resolves post-tumor resection.
Dent's disease involves mutations in CLCN5 affecting renal phosphate handling.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 1)

items.title: Redistributive Hypophosphatemia in Hospitalized Patients
items.content: Hypophosphatemia in hospitalized patients is often due to massive redistribution of phosphate from the extracellular fluid into cells, a phenomenon exemplified by insulin therapy for diabetic ketoacidosis. The severity correlates with prior depletion of phosphate and other electrolytes.
keyPoints: Insulin therapy can cause hypophosphatemia many hours after initiation.
Severity is related to antecedent depletion of phosphate and electrolytes.
Prerenal azotemia may obscure significant phosphate depletion at baseline.
Other contributing factors include starvation, IV glucose without nutrients, elevated catecholamines, respiratory alkalosis, and recovery from metabolic acidosis.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnostic Thresholds of Severe Hypophosphatemia
items.content: Severe hypophosphatemia results in generalized cellular energy metabolism defects due to ATP depletion, leading to tissue or organ dysfunction. Neuromuscular manifestations can be severe at phosphate concentrations <0.25 mmol/L.
keyPoints: Clinical manifestations include muscle weakness, lethargy, confusion, dysarthria, and seizures.
Serious sequelae like paralysis and seizures occur at phosphate levels <0.25 mmol/L (<0.8 mg/dL).
Rhabdomyolysis may develop with rapidly progressive hypophosphatemia.
Respiratory failure and cardiac dysfunction can occur at 0.5–0.8 mmol/L (1.5–2.5 mg/dL) and are reversible with phosphate treatment.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 2)

items.title: When and How to Treat Severe Hypophosphatemia
items.content: Severe hypophosphatemia, defined as serum phosphate levels <0.75 mmol/L (<2 mg/dL), requires prompt correction due to its dangerous nature. Intravenous (IV) therapy should be empirically determined based on renal function and severity of symptoms. Initial IV doses range from 0.2–0.8 mmol/kg of elemental phosphorus over 6 hours, with higher doses (>20 mmol/6 h) reserved for patients with serum levels <0.5 mmol/L (1.5 mg/dL) and normal renal function. Serum phosphate and calcium should be monitored every 6-12 hours to avoid complications.
keyPoints: Severe hypophosphatemia: <0.75 mmol/L (<2 mg/dL)
Initial IV doses: 0.2–0.8 mmol/kg of elemental phosphorus over 6 h
Higher IV doses (>20 mmol/6 h) for serum levels <0.5 mmol/L (1.5 mg/dL) with normal renal function
Monitor serum phosphate and calcium every 6-12 hours
Avoid serum calcium-phosphorus product >50 mg²/dL
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hypophosphatemia

items.title: Management of Chronic Hypophosphatemia
items.content: Chronic hypophosphatemia management depends on the underlying cause. Vitamin D deficiency-related hypophosphatemia responds to vitamin D and calcium treatment. Disorders like X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets (XLH) and tumor-induced osteomalacia (TIO) are managed with oral phosphate, sometimes supplemented by calcium and 1,25(OH)2D to prevent secondary hyperparathyroidism. Burosumab, an FGF23 inhibitor, is approved for XLH and TIO, improving symptoms like bone pain and healing fractures. Surgical removal of tumors is optimal for TIO when feasible.
keyPoints: Vitamin D deficiency: treat with vitamin D and calcium
XLH and TIO: manage with oral phosphate, calcium, 1,25(OH)2D supplements
Avoid simultaneous administration of oral calcium and phosphate
Burosumab approved for XLH and TIO
Surgical removal is optimal for TIO when tumors are localized
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hypophosphatemia

items.title: Hyperphosphatemia: Mechanisms and Clinical Implications
items.content: Hyperphosphatemia, defined as a fasting serum phosphate concentration >1.8 mmol/L (5.5 mg/dL) in adults, can result from impaired glomerular filtration, hypoparathyroidism, or excessive extracellular fluid phosphate loads. It is crucial to distinguish between hyperphosphatemia due to renal impairment and that caused by excessive phosphate delivery.
keyPoints: Hyperphosphatemia defined as >1.8 mmol/L (5.5 mg/dL) in adults
Causes include impaired glomerular filtration, hypoparathyroidism, and massive extracellular fluid phosphate loads
In chronic renal insufficiency, hyperphosphatemia contributes to secondary hyperparathyroidism
Hypoparathyroidism increases NaPi-2 co-transporter expression leading to hyperphosphatemia
Clinical consequences of severe hyperphosphatemia include hypocalcemia and calcium phosphate precipitates
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-3 Causes of Hyperphosphatemia

items.title: Renal Insufficiency and Secondary Hyperparathyroidism
items.content: In chronic renal insufficiency, reduced GFR leads to phosphate retention. This results in hyperphosphatemia, which further impairs renal synthesis of 1,25(OH)2D, increases FGF23 levels, and stimulates PTH secretion, contributing to secondary hyperparathyroidism.
keyPoints: Reduced GFR in chronic renal insufficiency leads to phosphate retention
Hyperphosphatemia impairs renal synthesis of 1,25(OH)2D
Increased FGF23 levels and PTH secretion contribute to secondary hyperparathyroidism
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-3 Causes of Hyperphosphatemia

items.title: Acute Severe Hyperphosphatemia: Clinical Consequences
items.content: Acute severe hyperphosphatemia can lead to widespread calcium phosphate precipitates and hypocalcemia, resulting in tetany, seizures, accelerated nephrocalcinosis, and pulmonary or cardiac calcifications. Serum phosphate levels can reach up to 7 mmol/L (20 mg/dL) in cases of massive soft tissue injury or tumor lysis syndrome.
keyPoints: Acute severe hyperphosphatemia causes calcium phosphate precipitates
Hypocalcemia leads to tetany, seizures, and nephrocalcinosis
Pulmonary or cardiac calcifications can occur
Serum phosphate levels may reach 7 mmol/L (20 mg/dL) in severe cases
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-3 Causes of Hyperphosphatemia

items.title: Management Strategies for Severe Hyperphosphatemia
items.content: In cases of severe hyperphosphatemia, especially with renal failure and symptomatic hypocalcemia, hemodialysis is the most effective therapeutic strategy. Volume expansion can enhance renal phosphate clearance, while aluminum hydroxide antacids or sevelamer may help chelate intestinal phosphate salts.
keyPoints: Hemodialysis is recommended early in severe hyperphosphatemia with renal failure and symptomatic hypocalcemia.
Volume expansion enhances renal phosphate clearance.
Aluminum hydroxide antacids or sevelamer can be used to chelate intestinal phosphate salts.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hyperphosphatemia | MAGNESIUM METABOLISM

items.title: Regulation of Serum Magnesium Concentrations
items.content: Serum magnesium levels are tightly regulated within 0.7–1 mmol/L (1.5–2 meq/L; 1.7–2.4 mg/dL). Renal reabsorption, particularly in the cTAL via a paracellular route, is crucial for maintaining these levels. PTH increases magnesium reabsorption in the cTAL, while hypercalcemia and hypermagnesemia inhibit it by activating the CaSR.
keyPoints: Serum magnesium concentration is regulated within 0.7–1 mmol/L (1.5–2 meq/L; 1.7–2.4 mg/dL).
60% of renal magnesium reabsorption occurs in the cTAL via a paracellular route.
PTH increases magnesium reabsorption in the cTAL, while hypercalcemia and hypermagnesemia inhibit it by activating the CaSR.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hyperphosphatemia | MAGNESIUM METABOLISM

items.title: Understanding the Mechanisms and Red Flags of Hypomagnesemia
items.content: Hypomagnesemia often indicates significant depletion of body magnesium stores, typically ranging from 0.5–1 mmol/kg. It can result from various causes including intestinal malabsorption, gastrointestinal losses, renal tubular defects, and shifts of magnesium into cells or bone. Rare genetic disorders also contribute to hypomagnesemia.
keyPoints: Hypomagnesemia signifies substantial depletion of body magnesium stores (0.5–1 mmol/kg).
Causes include intestinal malabsorption, protracted vomiting, diarrhea, defective renal tubular reabsorption, and rapid shifts into cells or bone.
Rare genetic disorders like primary infantile hypomagnesemia types 1 and 2, and mutations in TRPM6 gene can cause hypomagnesemia.
Proton pump inhibitors may induce hypomagnesemia without renal magnesium wasting.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOMAGNESEMIA | TABLE 421-4 Causes of Hypomagnesemia

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnostic Thresholds for Hypomagnesemia
items.content: Hypomagnesemia can lead to neuromuscular, cardiac, and psychiatric symptoms. Patients are usually asymptomatic when serum magnesium concentrations are above 0.5 mmol/L (1 meq/L; 1.2 mg/dL). Cardiac arrhythmias and ECG changes may occur, with enhanced sensitivity to digitalis toxicity.
keyPoints: Symptoms include tetany, seizures, muscle weakness, ataxia, nystagmus, vertigo, depression, irritability, psychosis.
Asymptomatic when serum magnesium >0.5 mmol/L (1 meq/L; 1.2 mg/dL).
Cardiac arrhythmias and ECG abnormalities like prolonged PR or QT intervals may occur.
Hypomagnesemia enhances sensitivity to digitalis toxicity.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOMAGNESEMIA | TABLE 421-4 Causes of Hypomagnesemia

items.title: Management and Correction of Hypomagnesemia
items.content: Correction of hypomagnesemia is crucial for resolving associated electrolyte abnormalities such as hypocalcemia and hypokalemia. Magnesium supplementation is necessary to correct these imbalances, especially when serum magnesium levels are very low (<0.4 mmol/L [<0.8 meq/L; <1 mg/dL]).
keyPoints: Hypomagnesemia correction is essential for resolving hypocalcemia and hypokalemia.
Magnesium supplementation required to correct electrolyte imbalances.
Very low serum magnesium (<0.4 mmol/L [<0.8 meq/L; <1 mg/dL]) can impair PTH secretion and synthesis of 1,25(OH)2D.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOMAGNESEMIA | TABLE 421-4 Causes of Hypomagnesemia

items.title: Management of Hypomagnesemia: Oral vs. Parenteral Treatment
items.content: In mild, asymptomatic hypomagnesemia, oral magnesium salts are recommended in divided doses totaling 20–30 mmol/d (40–60 meq/d). For more severe cases, parenteral treatment with IV MgCl₃ is preferred, especially if renal function is normal. The infusion rate should be adjusted based on GFR, and serum magnesium levels must be monitored every 12–24 hours.
keyPoints: Oral magnesium salts: 20–30 mmol/d (40–60 meq/d)
IV MgCl₃ for severe cases: 50 mmol/d (100 meq Mg²⁺/d) if renal function is normal
Adjust infusion rate by 50–75% if GFR is reduced
Monitor serum magnesium every 12–24 hours
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hypomagnesemia | HYPERMAGNESEMIA

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Hypermagnesemia: Clinical Manifestations and Causes
items.content: Hypermagnesemia is often associated with renal insufficiency, as normal kidneys can excrete up to 250 mmol/d of magnesium. It may also occur due to excessive reabsorption or massive exogenous exposure. Clinically, hypermagnesemia manifests at serum levels >2 mmol/L (>4 meq/L; >4.8 mg/dL) with symptoms like vasodilation, neuromuscular blockade, and hypotension.
keyPoints: Normal renal excretion: 250 mmol/d
Serum magnesium level indicating hypermagnesemia: >2 mmol/L (>4 meq/L; >4.8 mg/dL)
Clinical manifestations: vasodilation, neuromuscular blockade, hypotension
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hypomagnesemia | HYPERMAGNESEMIA

items.title: Identifying the Causes of Hypermagnesemia
items.content: Hypermagnesemia can result from excessive magnesium intake, rapid mobilization from soft tissues, impaired excretion due to renal failure, or other conditions like adrenal insufficiency. Recognizing these causes is crucial for effective management.
keyPoints: Excessive magnesium intake: Cathartics, urologic irrigants, parenteral administration
Rapid mobilization from soft tissues: Trauma, shock, sepsis, cardiac arrest, burns
Impaired excretion: Renal failure, familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia
Other causes: Adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism, hypothermia
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · TABLE 421-5 Causes of Hypermagnesemia | Hypermagnesemia

items.title: Management and Clinical Implications of Severe Hypermagnesemia
items.content: Severe hypermagnesemia, with serum levels >4 mmol/L, can lead to serious neurological and cardiovascular symptoms. Management includes interrupting magnesium sources, enhancing clearance, and using IV calcium for temporary symptom relief.
keyPoints: Serum magnesium levels >4 mmol/L: Hypoactive tendon reflexes, gastrointestinal hypomotility, facial flushing
Levels approaching 10 mmol/L: Asystole risk
Management: Identify/stop sources, IV hydration, hemodialysis for renal insufficiency
IV calcium (100–200 mg over 1–2 h): Temporary symptom improvement
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · TABLE 421-5 Causes of Hypermagnesemia | Hypermagnesemia

items.title: Vitamin D Synthesis and Metabolism: Key Pathways and Regulation
items.content: 1,25-Dihydroxyvitamin D [1,25(OH)₂D] is crucial for mineral ion homeostasis. Vitamin D synthesis begins with skin exposure to ultraviolet radiation converting 7-dehydrocholesterol into vitamin D. This process can be inhibited by melanin and sunblocks. Dietary sources in North America include fortified cereals and dairy products. Vitamin D enters circulation bound to vitamin D-binding protein, undergoes hydroxylation in the liver to form 25-hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D], which is further hydroxylated in the kidney by PTH-induced enzymes to produce active hormone. The enzyme 1α-hydroxylase is also found in other cells like macrophages, where it can cause hypercalcemia without regulation by calcium or 1,25(OH)₂D.
keyPoints: Vitamin D synthesis begins with ultraviolet radiation converting 7-dehydrocholesterol to vitamin D.
Cutaneous production of vitamin D is decreased by melanin and sunblocks.
In the US/Canada, dietary sources include fortified cereals and dairy products.
25(OH)D circulates bound to vitamin D-binding protein (88%), with a half-life of ~2–3 weeks.
1α-hydroxylase in kidneys is regulated by PTH and hypophosphatemia; repressed by calcium, FGF23, and 1,25(OH)₂D₃.
Macrophages' 1α-hydroxylase activity can cause hypercalcemia without regulation by calcium or 1,25(OH)₂D.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · SYNTHESIS AND METABOLISM

items.title: Regulation of Vitamin D Metabolism: Inactivation and Disorders
items.content: The inactivation of vitamin D metabolites is primarily mediated by the 24-hydroxylase enzyme, which is induced by 1,25(OH)₂D itself, creating a feedback loop. FGF23 also induces this enzyme, reducing circulating levels of active vitamin D. Mutations in CYP24A1 can lead to infantile hypercalcemia and long-term complications like nephrocalcinosis. In diseases affecting the terminal ileum, impaired recirculation of polar metabolites results in accelerated losses of vitamin D.
keyPoints: Vitamin D 24-hydroxylase is induced by 1,25(OH)₂D and FGF23, promoting its own inactivation.
Mutations in CYP24A1 can cause infantile hypercalcemia and long-term nephrocalcinosis.
Impaired recirculation of vitamin D metabolites occurs with terminal ileum diseases.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · SYNTHESIS AND METABOLISM

items.title: Clinical Implications of Vitamin D Metabolism in Disease States
items.content: In conditions like sarcoidosis and lymphoma, macrophages produce 1α-hydroxylase leading to hypercalcemia. Treatment with glucocorticoids or ketoconazole can reduce 1,25(OH)₂D production and lower serum calcium in sarcoidosis but not in lymphoma. Understanding the regulation of vitamin D metabolism is crucial for managing these conditions.
keyPoints: Macrophages in granulomas produce 1α-hydroxylase leading to hypercalcemia, unregulated by calcium or 1,25(OH)₂D.
Sarcoidosis-associated hypercalcemia can be treated with glucocorticoids or ketoconazole.
Chloroquine does not lower elevated serum 1,25(OH)₂D levels in lymphoma.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · SYNTHESIS AND METABOLISM

items.title: High Affinity and Specificity of VDR for 1,25(OH)₂D
items.content: The vitamin D receptor (VDR) has a significantly higher affinity for 1,25(OH)₂D compared to other vitamin D metabolites. This high specificity is crucial for its biological effects.
keyPoints: Affinity of VDR for 1,25(OH)₂D is approximately three orders of magnitude higher than for other vitamin D metabolites.
Ensures specific receptor-dependent actions under normal physiological conditions.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · ACTIONS OF 1,25(OH),D

items.title: Role of 1,25(OH)₂D in Calcium and Phosphate Homeostasis
items.content: 1,25(OH)₂D is a major inducer of calbindin 9K and calcium transporters TRPV5 and TRPV6, enhancing intestinal calcium absorption. It also regulates phosphate homeostasis by inducing FGF23 expression.
keyPoints: Induces calbindin 9K in the intestine for active calcium transport.
Upregulates TRPV5 and TRPV6 to increase intestinal calcium absorption efficiency.
Regulates phosphate homeostasis via induction of FGF23 expression.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · ACTIONS OF 1,25(OH),D

items.title: Antiproliferative Effects of 1,25(OH)₂D on Parathyroid and Cancer Cells
items.content: 1,25(OH)₂D exerts antiproliferative effects on parathyroid cells by suppressing PTH gene transcription. It also impacts various cancer cell types, including breast and prostate.
keyPoints: Suppresses transcription of the parathyroid hormone gene in parathyroid glands.
Antiproliferative effects observed in keratinocytes, breast cancer cells, and prostate cancer cells.
VDR acts as a transcriptional repressor in keratinocytes.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · ACTIONS OF 1,25(OH),D

items.title: Prevalence and Risk Factors of Vitamin D Deficiency
items.content: Vitamin D deficiency is prevalent in the United States, with more than 29% prevalence among obese children. The elderly and nursing home residents are at higher risk due to decreased vitamin D synthesis and absorption.
keyPoints: Prevalence >29% in obese children (NHANES III).
Elderly and nursing home residents have increased risk.
Decreased efficiency of vitamin D synthesis with age.
Impaired enterohepatic circulation in terminal ileal disease.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms and Consequences of Vitamin D Deficiency
items.content: Deficiency can result from insufficient production, dietary intake, accelerated loss, impaired activation, or resistance to vitamin D. It leads to hypocalcemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, osteopenia, and proximal myopathy.
keyPoints: Causes include deficient production, lack of intake, accelerated losses, impaired activation, or resistance.
Results in hypocalcemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, osteopenia (decreased bone mineral density), and proximal myopathy.
Associated with increased overall mortality, including cardiovascular causes.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Disorders Related to Vitamin D Deficiency
items.content: Pseudovitamin D–deficiency rickets and hereditary vitamin D-resistant rickets are genetic disorders affecting vitamin D metabolism, presenting with growth retardation, rickets, hypocalcemic seizures, and alopecia.
keyPoints: Pseudovitamin D–deficiency rickets (vitamin D-dependent rickets type I) is autosomal recessive, presents in the first year of life, requires lifelong therapy.
Hereditary vitamin D-resistant rickets (type II) involves VDR mutations, presenting with alopecia and requiring calcium/phosphate infusions.
Serum 1,25(OH)₂D levels are low in type I despite normal 25(OH)D levels; elevated in type II due to impaired inactivation.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 1)

items.title: Mechanism of Rickets in Vitamin D Deficiency
items.content: In children, vitamin D deficiency leads to rickets by causing impaired apoptosis of late hypertrophic chondrocytes and subsequent expansion of the growth plate. This is due to hypophosphatemia resulting from secondary hyperparathyroidism.
keyPoints: Rickets results in an expanded hypertrophic chondrocyte layer due to impaired apoptosis.
Hypophosphatemia, a consequence of secondary hyperparathyroidism, is key in rachitic growth plate development.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Features and Treatment of Osteomalacia
items.content: Osteomalacia results from impaired mineralization due to hypocalcemia and hypophosphatemia, leading to bone fragility. Vitamin D and calcium supplementation can reduce hip fractures in the elderly.
keyPoints: Hypocalcemia and hypophosphatemia impair bone matrix mineralization.
Proximal myopathy resolves rapidly with vitamin D treatment.
Vitamin D and calcium supplementation decrease hip fracture incidence.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis of Vitamin D Deficiency
items.content: Serum $25(OH)D$ levels are the specific test for vitamin D deficiency. Levels <37 nmol/L (<15 ng/mL) indicate increasing PTH and lower bone density, while sufficiency is defined as >50 nmol/L (>20 ng/mL).
keyPoints: Specific screening test: serum $25(OH)D$ level.
Deficient levels: <$37 	ext{ nmol/L}$ (<15 ng/mL), associated with increased PTH and lower bone density.
Sufficiency defined as >50 nmol/L (>20 ng/mL).
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 2)

items.subtopic: VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency
items.title: Radiologic Features of Vitamin D Deficiency in Children
items.content: Radiologic features of vitamin D deficiency in children include widened, expanded growth plates characteristic of rickets. These are seen in long bones and at the costochondral junctions, leading to 'rachitic rosary.' Impaired intramembranous bone mineralization results in delayed fusion of calvarial sutures and decreased radiopacity of cortical bone.
keyPoints: Widened, expanded growth plates characteristic of rickets
'Rachitic rosary' at costochondral junctions
Delayed fusion of calvarial sutures
Decreased radiopacity of cortical bone in long bones
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency

items.subtopic: VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency
items.title: Recommended Daily Intake and Supplementation Strategies for Vitamin D
items.content: The recommended daily intake of vitamin D is 600 IU from ages 1 to 70, and 800 IU for those over 70. Supplementation with 800 IU plus calcium decreases hip fracture risk in elderly women. In older adults with adequate levels, doses above the RDI do not improve bone density or prevent falls. Small daily doses (400 IU) are effective in preventing fractures and falls compared to large intermittent doses.
keyPoints: 600 IU recommended for ages 1-70
800 IU recommended for those over 70
800 IU with calcium reduces hip fracture risk
Small daily doses (400 IU) prevent fractures/falls better than large bolus doses
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency

items.subtopic: VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency
items.title: Treatment and Monitoring of Vitamin D Deficiency
items.content: Treat vitamin D deficiency with pharmacologic repletion initially (50,000 IU weekly for 3–12 weeks), followed by maintenance therapy (800 IU daily). Use active metabolites like calcitriol or doxercalciferol if 1α-hydroxylation is impaired. Monitor treatment efficacy with serum and urinary calcium measurements; normocalcemia should occur within a week, but PTH and alkaline phosphatase may take longer to normalize.
keyPoints: Initial repletion: 50,000 IU weekly for 3–12 weeks
Maintenance therapy: 800 IU daily
Use calcitriol or doxercalciferol if 1α-hydroxylation is impaired
Monitor with serum and urinary calcium; normocalcemia within a week
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency

items.question: What is the recommended daily intake of vitamin D for individuals aged 1 to 70 years according to the National Academy of Medicine 2010 report?
items.explanation: The recommended daily intake is 600 IU for individuals aged 1 to 70 years.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency

items.subtopic: VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency
items.question: Which of the following statements about vitamin D supplementation in older adults is true according to the VITAL trial?
options: Daily small doses can prevent fractures and falls.
Large intermittent bolus doses are more effective than daily small doses.
Vitamin D levels above recommended intake further improve bone mineral density.
items.explanation: The VITAL trial showed that daily small doses of vitamin D can prevent fractures and falls in older adults.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency

items.subtopic: VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency
items.question: What is the typical range for 24-hour urinary calcium excretion in patients who are vitamin D replete and taking adequate calcium supplementation?
items.explanation: The typical range for 24-hour urinary calcium excretion is 100–250 mg/24 h.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency

items.subtopic: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)
items.statement: Osteogenesis imperfecta is caused by single amino acid substitutions in the helical portion of type I collagen.
items.explanation: Single amino acid substitutions in COL1A1 or COL1A2 disrupt bone organization, causing osteogenesis imperfecta.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)

items.subtopic: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)
items.statement: Osteoblasts are derived from cells of epithelial origin.
items.explanation: Osteoblasts are derived from mesenchymal, not epithelial, cell origins.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)

items.statement: Osteoclasts are derived from the mesenchymal lineage.
items.explanation: Osteoclasts derive from the hematopoietic lineage, not the mesenchymal lineage.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 2)

items.statement: Indian hedgehog is important for osteoblast development in endochondral ossification.
items.explanation: Ihh-deficient mice lack osteoblasts in the type of bone formed on a cartilage mold, indicating its role in osteoblast development.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 2)

items.statement: The Haversian systems are formed by deposition of lamellar bone in a concentric arrangement around blood vessels.
items.explanation: In long bones, the deposition of lamellar bone in a concentric arrangement around blood vessels forms the Haversian systems.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 3)

items.statement: Osteocalcin is made by both osteoblasts and other cell types.
items.explanation: Osteocalcin is a protein secreted from osteoblasts and is made virtually only by osteoblasts.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 3)

items.statement: The ionized calcium concentration in the extracellular fluid is maintained within a narrow range because of its critical role in cellular functions.
items.explanation: Ionized calcium must be tightly regulated due to its essential roles in neuromuscular activity, secretion, and signal transduction.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 1)

items.statement: Intracellular cytosolic free calcium levels are higher than ionized calcium concentration in the blood.
items.explanation: Cytosolic free calcium levels (~100 nmol/L) are much lower than ionized calcium concentration in the blood (1.1–1.3 mmol/L).
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 1)

items.statement: Optimal rates of calcium absorption require gastric acid.
items.explanation: Gastric acid is especially important for the absorption of weakly dissociable calcium supplements like calcium carbonate.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 2)

items.statement: The renal CaSR does not influence renal calcium reabsorption.
items.explanation: The renal CaSR controls renal calcium reabsorption independently of PTH or 1,25(OH)₂D.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 2)

items.statement: Phosphate absorption is inhibited by large doses of calcium salts.
items.explanation: Large doses of calcium salts can inhibit phosphate absorption.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · PHOSPHORUS METABOLISM

items.statement: Serum phosphate levels are constant throughout the day, unaffected by food intake or circadian rhythms.
items.explanation: Serum phosphate levels vary significantly due to food intake and circadian rhythms.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · PHOSPHORUS METABOLISM

items.statement: Inadequate intestinal phosphate absorption is a common cause of hypophosphatemia in modern clinical practice.
items.explanation: Inadequate intestinal phosphate absorption is almost never observed now that aluminum hydroxide antacids are no longer widely used.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 1)

items.statement: Tumor-induced osteomalacia syndrome is associated with elevated FGF23 levels.
items.explanation: Tumors in tumor-induced osteomalacia syndrome secrete FGF23, leading to hypophosphatemia.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 1)

items.statement: Hypophosphatemia-induced rhabdomyolysis can be overlooked because creatine phosphokinase elevations peak after serum phosphate levels begin to normalize.
items.explanation: Creatine phosphokinase elevations may peak 1–2 days after the nadir in serum phosphate.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 2)

items.statement: Respiratory failure and cardiac dysfunction due to hypophosphatemia are irreversible even with phosphate treatment.
items.explanation: Respiratory failure and cardiac dysfunction may occur at certain serum phosphate levels but are reversible with phosphate treatment.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 2)

items.statement: Complete normalization of hypophosphatemia is generally possible in conditions like XLH and TIO.
items.explanation: The passage states that complete normalization of hypophosphatemia is generally not possible in these conditions.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hypophosphatemia

items.statement: Thiazide diuretics may be used to prevent nephrocalcinosis in patients with renal tubular disorders.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that thiazide diuretics can be used to prevent nephrocalcinosis in such patients.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hypophosphatemia

items.statement: Hyperphosphatemia is always caused by impaired renal function.
items.explanation: Hyperphosphatemia can also result from excessive delivery of phosphate into the ECF or other causes.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-3 Causes of Hyperphosphatemia

items.statement: Tumoral calcinosis is associated with elevated serum phosphorus levels in all cases.
items.explanation: In some forms of tumoral calcinosis, serum phosphorus levels are normal.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-3 Causes of Hyperphosphatemia

items.statement: Serum magnesium levels accurately reflect total body magnesium stores.
items.explanation: Only 1% of body magnesium resides in the extracellular fluid, so serum measurements may not accurately reflect total body magnesium stores.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hyperphosphatemia | MAGNESIUM METABOLISM

items.statement: PTH increases magnesium reabsorption in the cortical thick ascending limb.
items.explanation: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) increases magnesium reabsorption in the cTAL.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hyperphosphatemia | MAGNESIUM METABOLISM

items.statement: Dietary magnesium deficiency is a common cause of hypomagnesemia.
items.explanation: Dietary magnesium deficiency is unlikely except in cases like alcoholism.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOMAGNESEMIA | TABLE 421-4 Causes of Hypomagnesemia

items.statement: Proton pump inhibitors can cause hypomagnesemia through renal wasting.
items.explanation: The mechanism by which proton pump inhibitors cause hypomagnesemia does not involve renal wasting.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOMAGNESEMIA | TABLE 421-4 Causes of Hypomagnesemia

items.statement: Normal kidneys can excrete large amounts (250 mmol/d) of magnesium, making hypermagnesemia rare without renal insufficiency.
items.explanation: Hypermagnesemia is rarely seen in the absence of renal insufficiency because normal kidneys can excrete large amounts of magnesium.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hypomagnesemia | HYPERMAGNESEMIA

items.statement: IV MgSO₄ administration may cause hypercalcemia due to its sulfate anions binding calcium in serum and urine.
items.explanation: IV MgSO₄ may aggravate hypocalcemia, not hypercalcemia, because the sulfate anions can bind calcium.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Hypomagnesemia | HYPERMAGNESEMIA

items.statement: Hemodialysis is an effective treatment for hypermagnesemia in patients with significant renal insufficiency.
items.explanation: Hemodialysis can effectively remove excess magnesium, especially in patients with renal insufficiency.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · TABLE 421-5 Causes of Hypermagnesemia | Hypermagnesemia

items.statement: Hypercalcemia is a direct result of hypermagnesemia.
items.explanation: Hypermagnesemia causes hypocalcemia, not hypercalcemia.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · TABLE 421-5 Causes of Hypermagnesemia | Hypermagnesemia

items.statement: Vitamin D from plant sources and animal sources have different biological activities.
items.explanation: Both vitamin D2 (from plants) and vitamin D3 (from animals) have equivalent biological activity.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · SYNTHESIS AND METABOLISM

items.statement: The half-life of 25-hydroxyvitamin D is significantly reduced when levels of vitamin D-binding protein are decreased.
items.explanation: The half-life of 25(OH)D is greatly shortened when vitamin D-binding protein levels are reduced.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · SYNTHESIS AND METABOLISM

items.statement: The VDR is expressed only in tissues involved in mineral ion homeostasis.
items.explanation: The VDR is also expressed in tissues not related to mineral ion homeostasis, such as keratinocytes and certain cancer cells.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · ACTIONS OF 1,25(OH),D

items.statement: 1,25(OH)₂D has antiproliferative effects on parathyroid cells.
items.explanation: 1,25(OH)₂D suppresses the transcription of the parathyroid hormone gene and has antiproliferative effects on parathyroid cells.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · ACTIONS OF 1,25(OH),D

items.statement: Mild to moderate vitamin D deficiency is typically asymptomatic.
items.explanation: The passage states that mild to moderate vitamin D deficiency is usually asymptomatic.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 1)

items.statement: Hypocalcemia in long-standing vitamin D deficiency often presents with acute symptoms like numbness and tingling.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that hypocalcemia rarely presents with acute symptoms unless there is concurrent hypomagnesemia or bisphosphonate use.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 1)

items.statement: In severe vitamin D deficiency, levels of the active hormone $1,25(OH)_2D$ are often low.
items.explanation: Paradoxically, levels of this hormone are often normal in severe vitamin D deficiency.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 2)

items.statement: Hypophosphatemia is a key etiologic factor in the development of the rachitic growth plate.
items.explanation: Investigations in murine models demonstrate that hypophosphatemia, which in vitamin D deficiency is a consequence of secondary hyperparathyroidism, is a key etiologic factor in the development of the rachitic growth plate.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 2)

items.subtopic: VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency
items.statement: Polymorphisms in the CYP3A4 gene can lead to a need for higher doses of vitamin D supplementation.
items.explanation: CYP3A4 polymorphisms induce strong catabolism of vitamin D metabolites, requiring higher doses.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency

items.subtopic: VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency
items.statement: Pharmacologic repletion for severe vitamin D deficiency is typically administered as a single dose.
items.explanation: Severe vitamin D deficiency is treated with pharmacologic repletion initially at 50,000 IU weekly for 3–12 weeks.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency

items.subtopic: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)
items.assertion: Runx2 is essential for osteoblast development.
items.reason: Runx2 regulates the expression of several important osteoblast proteins.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why Runx2 is essential.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)

items.subtopic: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)
items.assertion: Osteoclasts synthesize bone matrix.
items.reason: Osteoblasts are responsible for synthesizing and secreting the organic matrix of bone.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because osteoclasts resorb, not synthesize, bone matrix; the reason is true.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease | BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 1)

items.assertion: Estradiol decreases osteoclast number and bone resorption.
items.reason: Estradiol acts on multiple cellular targets in bone, including osteoclasts.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and estradiol's action on osteoclasts is a correct explanation for its effect.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 2)

items.assertion: Osteocytes directly resorb bone through a process called osteoclastic resorption.
items.reason: Osteocytes use molecular machinery similar to that in osteoclasts for mineral and matrix dissolution.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; osteocytes cause bone resorption through osteocytic osteolysis, not osteoclastic resorption.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 2)

items.assertion: Bone remodeling occurs along lines of force generated by mechanical stress.
items.reason: Osteocytes sense signals from mechanical stresses and transmit them to osteoclasts and osteoblasts or their precursors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 3)

items.assertion: Expanding lesions in bone such as tumors induce resorption by producing ligands that stimulate osteoclast differentiation.
items.reason: Tumors produce PTHrP, which stimulates osteoclast differentiation and function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · BONE STRUCTURE AND METABOLISM (part 3)

items.assertion: Sustained low calcium intake leads to increased bone resorption.
items.reason: Increased blood levels of PTH and 1,25(OH)₂D activate osteoclastic bone resorption.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 2)

items.assertion: Paracellin-1 is involved in calcium reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubules.
items.reason: Calcium reabsorption in the cTAL requires paracellin-1 and Claudin14.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; paracellin-1 is involved in calcium reabsorption in the cTAL, not the distal convoluted tubules.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · CALCIUM METABOLISM (part 2)

items.assertion: FGF23 is a major regulator of renal phosphate excretion.
items.reason: FGF23 inhibits the expression and activity of Na⁺/PO₄²⁻ co-transporters in the proximal tubule.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · PHOSPHORUS METABOLISM

items.assertion: Hypocalcemia enhances renal phosphate reabsorption.
items.reason: Renal phosphate reabsorption is impaired by hypocalcemia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; hypocalcemia impairs, not enhances, renal phosphate reabsorption.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · PHOSPHORUS METABOLISM

items.assertion: Chronic hypophosphatemia usually signifies the presence of a persistent renal tubular phosphate-wasting disorder.
items.reason: Excessive activation of PTH/PTHrP receptors in the proximal tubule is a common cause of renal hypophosphatemia.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not fully explain the assertion as other causes exist.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 1)

items.assertion: Neutralizing antibodies against FGF23 can be used to treat hypophosphatemia in patients with X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets.
items.reason: Patients with TIO exhibit elevations of FGF23 in their blood.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason supports the use of neutralizing antibodies against FGF23.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-1 Causes of Hypophosphatemia (part 1)

items.assertion: Hyperphosphatemia can result from hypoparathyroidism.
items.reason: Hypoparathyroidism leads to increased expression of NaPi-2 co-transporters in the proximal tubule.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-3 Causes of Hyperphosphatemia

items.assertion: Tumoral calcinosis is caused by activating mutations in the FGF23 sequence.
items.reason: These mutations lead to low levels of active FGF23 in the bloodstream.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; tumoral calcinosis involves inactivating mutations.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA | TABLE 421-3 Causes of Hyperphosphatemia

items.assertion: Hypomagnesemia can cause cardiac arrhythmias.
items.reason: Cardiac arrhythmias may occur due to prolonged PR or QT intervals and T-wave changes associated with hypomagnesemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOMAGNESEMIA | TABLE 421-4 Causes of Hypomagnesemia

items.assertion: Patients with serum magnesium levels >0.5 mmol/L are usually symptomatic.
items.reason: Symptoms may not correlate well with serum magnesium levels, and patients are often asymptomatic when levels are above this threshold.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; patients are usually asymptomatic at these levels.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · HYPOMAGNESEMIA | TABLE 421-4 Causes of Hypomagnesemia

items.assertion: Hypomotility or ileus can be a finding in hypermagnesemia.
items.reason: Hypermagnesemia affects gastrointestinal motility leading to hypomotility or ileus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · TABLE 421-5 Causes of Hypermagnesemia | Hypermagnesemia

items.assertion: Mutations in the gene encoding vitamin D 24-hydroxylase can lead to hypercalcemia.
items.reason: The enzyme is responsible for promoting its own inactivation, thus mutations can result in elevated levels of active hormone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why mutations can cause hypercalcemia.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · SYNTHESIS AND METABOLISM

items.assertion: Keratinocyte production of 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D contributes to circulating levels of this hormone.
items.reason: The enzyme 25-hydroxyvitamin D-1α-hydroxylase is expressed in epidermal keratinocytes.
items.explanation: While the reason is true, the assertion is false because keratinocyte production does not contribute to circulating levels.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · SYNTHESIS AND METABOLISM

items.assertion: 1,25(OH)₂D induces bone resorption.
items.reason: It increases the expression of RANK ligand in osteoblasts, promoting osteoclast differentiation and activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the mechanism by which 1,25(OH)₂D induces bone resorption.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · ACTIONS OF 1,25(OH),D

items.assertion: Vitamin D deficiency leads to alopecia.
items.reason: The VDR in keratinocytes is primarily a transcriptional repressor and its effects on hair follicles are ligand-independent.
items.explanation: Alopecia occurs with mutant VDRs, not vitamin D deficiency; thus, the assertion is false.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · ACTIONS OF 1,25(OH),D

items.assertion: Patients with pseudovitamin D–deficiency rickets have low serum levels of 1,25(OH)₂D.
items.reason: This is due to mutations in the renal 1α-hydroxylase enzyme.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with pseudovitamin D–deficiency rickets have low serum levels of 1,25(OH)₂D.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 1)

items.assertion: Hereditary vitamin D-resistant rickets is caused by mutations in the gene responsible for 25-hydroxylation.
items.reason: This results in elevated serum levels of 1,25(OH)₂D due to impaired receptor function.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because hereditary vitamin D-resistant rickets is caused by mutations in the vitamin D receptor, not the gene for 25-hydroxylation.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 1)

items.assertion: Osteomalacia can occur even with normal calcium and phosphate levels.
items.reason: Chronic acidosis and drugs such as bisphosphonates can lead to osteomalacia by preventing the formation of the inorganic calcium/phosphate mineral phase of bone.
items.explanation: Even in the presence of normal calcium and phosphate levels, chronic acidosis and drugs such as bisphosphonates can lead to osteomalacia.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 2)

items.assertion: PTH-induced hypophosphatemia exacerbates the mineralization defect in the skeleton.
items.reason: PTH decreases urinary calcium excretion while promoting phosphaturia, leading to hypophosphatemia.
items.explanation: This results in hypophosphatemia, which exacerbates the mineralization defect in the skeleton.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 2)

items.subtopic: VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency
items.assertion: Pseudofractures are a specific radiologic feature of osteomalacia.
items.reason: They occur where large arteries contact the underlying skeletal elements, leading to radiolucencies due to arterial pulsations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why pseudofractures are a feature of osteomalacia.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency

items.subtopic: VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency
items.assertion: Normocalcemia is usually observed within 1 week after starting vitamin D therapy.
items.reason: Increases in PTH and alkaline phosphatase levels persist for 3–6 months.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why normocalcemia is observed within a week.
items.reference: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease · VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (part 3) | Vitamin D Deficiency
